symptoms.symptom_name: Intensely pruritic firm flat-topped violaceous polygonal papules symmetrically on the flexor wrists and anterior ankles

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cutaneous and oral lichen planus (acute flare)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The 'six Ps' — pruritic, purple, polygonal, planar papules — in a symmetrical flexor-wrist/ankle distribution are the hallmark of lichen planus.
Violaceous flat-topped flexural papules are not the salmon-pink, silvery-scaled extensor plaques of psoriasis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Fine white reticulated network on the papule surface (Wickham striae) on dermatoscopy

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cutaneous and oral lichen planus (acute flare)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Wickham striae are highly specific for lichen planus; demonstrating them requires dermatoscopy, so this sign is sealed from the AI Doctor, which reasons from the visible violaceous flat-topped papules instead.

symptoms.symptom_name: New linear papules along scratch lines (Koebner/isomorphic phenomenon)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cutaneous and oral lichen planus (acute flare)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Koebnerisation is characteristic of lichen planus (and psoriasis) and supports an isomorphic inflammatory dermatosis over an infestation or infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Non-erosive lacy white (reticular) buccal mucosal plaques without ulceration

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cutaneous and oral lichen planus (acute flare)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reticular buccal plaques are a classic mucosal manifestation of lichen planus; the non-erosive, non-painful quality argues against the emergency erosive form and against a severe drug reaction.

symptoms.symptom_name: No new/culprit medication, no known hepatitis C

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: lichenoid drug eruption / hepatitis-C-associated lichen planus
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a new culprit drug and of known hepatitis C argues against a lichenoid drug eruption or Hep-C-associated disease, though Hep-C serology is still reasonable given the association.
Severe cutaneous adverse reactions are overwhelmingly drug-triggered; no new drug substantially lowers the pre-test probability.

symptoms.symptom_name: No widespread blistering, skin peeling, skin pain, target lesions, systemic upset or fever

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The absence of blistering, skin pain, mucosal erosion, target lesions and systemic upset argues strongly against a severe cutaneous adverse reaction and supports community management with safety-netting.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clinician-measured vital signs (BP 135/85, HR 92, Temp 36.8, RR 16, SpO2 98%, Wt 75 kg)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal-range, afebrile vitals fit a systemically-well patient with a benign dermatosis; these are clinician-measured and sealed from the AI Doctor, which infers systemic wellness from the patient-reported absence of fever and feeling unwell.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clear nails and scalp, no generalised erythema, blistering or lymphadenopathy on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cutaneous and oral lichen planus (acute flare)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Localised disease with clear nails/scalp and no systemic signs supports limited, non-aggressive lichen planus; full nail/scalp grading requires in-person examination and is sealed from the AI Doctor.

symptoms.symptom_name: Deep intractable pruritus out of proportion to the rash with 48 hours of sleep deprivation

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cutaneous and oral lichen planus (acute flare)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pruritus out of proportion to the visible eruption is characteristic of lichen planus and drives the symptom burden, but reflects quality-of-life impact rather than clinical acuity.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Hepatitis C serology and baseline bloods (FBC, LFTs)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Lichen planus has a recognised association with hepatitis C; baseline bloods and Hep-C serology are reasonable at a prescriber review, and LFTs are prudent if a systemic corticosteroid is planned. Requires an in-person/prescriber pathway and does not need to precede symptom relief.
Punch biopsy confirms the diagnosis where clinically uncertain and is arranged by the GP/dermatologist; not required to begin first-line topical treatment for a clinically classic eruption.
Not indicated in a systemically-well, afebrile patient with a classic non-infective lichenoid eruption; ordering them reflects over-investigation.
